speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, Frieda Stieglitz. So, how did you manage to survive during this time? Were you going back and forth for the miles to go from the train station back to the end of your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the pleasant part, because it gave me something to do. But I was afraid. The worst part was being afraid. Somebody shouldn't give me away, somebody shouldn't see me. Shouldn't give me. That was the worst part of it. Not the hunger. If I was hungry, I didn't feel it so much. Because my stomach, I think-- I was very small before the war. I didn't want to eat. So my stomach was not-- in other words, stretched. In other words, the more you were before the war, the more you ate before the war, the harder it was to get used to the hunger. Since I ate very little as a child, when the war came, my stomach shrank more, so that I didn't require much food. Very minimum.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why, I think, I never got sick, because the fresh air, I think, helped me. The fresh air, being not in the city, being the-- there was no pollution in that village, and the fresh air helped me, you know? It's-- fresh air is very beneficial. You know? And the air was pure. There was very little pollution, like I said, and the food? I didn't require much. And apparently, my body was very strong, because I did not catch diseases or colds during that time, which is, I think a miracle in itself. I never caught a cold once until the war ended. The year the war ended, I caught my first cold, very bad cold. I remember it like today. And so it shows you, there was something going on. I don't know. Because how can you not catch a cold in two years in bitter winter? The snow-- anybody who went through the war, those-- the war years in Poland remembers they were one of the bitterest colds. Anybody can tell you. The snows were higher and colder. There were more soldiers killed because of the cold, and even though they had clothing. And I had very little clothing. And yet-- What did you have? Did you have anything for winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. You know what? I think I lost my-- I don't know if my coat got too small. I don't remember even wearing a coat. I remember wearing a shawl. Somebody gave me a shawl. I wore it, and I wrapped that around me. I wore-- it looked like just like any of the, like, little-- you know, like, from the village people. Like, you know, they-- they have the shawls around them. They don't wear coats. They wear shawls. And I put-- wrapped this around me. And I remember one something distinctly. One time, I was walking in the bitter snow, and a man came out who was my barber before the war. He was my barber. In other words, my mother used to hire him as the barber to cut our hair every so often when our hair got a little too long. And we would all sit down on a high chair. He would come to the house, and he would put a little chair on the chair, and we all sat down, had our haircuts. And this man, one day as I passed by his house, which was an area I wasn't familiar, he came out, and he had such pity on me when he realized. I think he must have recognized me. And he said-- because he knew my face. He saw my face every time he cut my hair. You don't forget a face you see every time. He must have known. And when he saw me, even bundled up, he recognized me. He said, come in. And he gave me some kind of shoes. Even though they didn't fit me perfectly, but he gave me something to-- to-- I remember he had such pity on me. I had, by that time, I think, wrapped something around me. I looked in a sorry state, I tell you. And yet, I didn't catch a cold. And yet, you know, the cold somehow didn't affect me. It-- it affected-- I was cold some, but you know, I would walk in this house, I would ask to come in, and they would let me in. They had pity on a little girl. And they didn't give me away. That was the miracle of it. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever come into contact with other people that were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one time it happened that I walked in the woods, in a certain part of woods, which was pretty far from Grodzisko already. It was a different village, it was-- and I knew my surroundings so well that I could walk for miles or kilometers out simple, and I could find my way and walk back easily, like you walk-- you know, your address. That's how I knew my surroundings. That's how I learned. By-- by-- It was only by trial-- excuse me. By trial and error. It was just my own-- you know, as I walked, I learned my surroundings. And far away. And I reached in these woods, it was very far. I don't even remember the village. And there were deep woods connecting. From there, you could go into woods that even Germans didn't go there. It was deep, deep woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know where they ended. I don't-- who knows? And it was hilly in the woods, and I was even afraid. There were animals. I was afraid to go deep, very deep. I was afraid to go in. I was alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I was walking a little bit, picking, like, blueberries or something, because I learned how to pick blueberries near-- not far from the edge. So I started picking blueberries, and I heard deep-- I heard voices. And they happened to be Jewish people. And I didn't talk to them, because I was so scared of them, I didn't know what to say. I didn't know what to say to them. They was-- I think they were afraid of me, and I was afraid of them. It was almost-- I wasn't used to any more people. I was so used to being alone, that it was easier for me-- for me to walk alone through the woods than to meet a person. That's how-- how unused to I got to people. This is-- I felt at peace by myself. I would walk and look up in the sky and the sun and the fields-- I mean, and the woods and the trees, and I felt they were my friends. But the people, I felt, were my enemies. And I didn't even know how to connect to my own people. I didn't know what to say. I was so unprepared to meet another human being. I just knew that I have to survive, but I didn't know how to relate to other people anymore. I-- because I was afraid, and I knew they were afraid to speak to me because I shouldn't say something in case I should be caught or something. So I understood all this in my mind, and I just-- I just functioned as a person. Me, alone, and that's it. I didn't need-- all I needed was a piece of bread to survive, a glass of milk or that, so I went to some of the goyim, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about your grandmother during that time, or wonder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, sure. I missed them so much that you cannot even imagine. I dreamt terrible dreams. I was dreaming about my mother, and I was dreaming about my grandmother, and I was dreaming terrible dreams. Nightmares I had every night, and sometimes I was told I screamed, because when I got with other people, they told me I screamed. And it was-- I was always dreaming that the Nazis were coming after me. And I was-- I was somehow managed to get away from, outsmart them, just like I-- conscious, I knew I must do this, and subconscious. I-- in other words, in the dreams, I had the same dreams what I was thinking during the day. The-- the nightmares was terrible in those days. I thought they will never leave me. It was-- oh, this was another terrible thing. At night, I had terrible nightmares. This lasted for many years. Many years. And my mother, when I did dream, she didn't dream appear like I knew her. She was so veiled, and black veiled. But I-- I knew it was my mother, and yet she-- I couldn't see her face. It was so terrible when I did see her presence. I knew it was her presence, but I couldn't recognize her. It was so terrible, the dreams. My lovely, beautiful mother. She was veiled and black. What-- what it means, till today, I don't understand, but I-- but it was terrible. Every dream I had was a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you go on like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back and forth, trying to find a way-- how long was the period of time that you were doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: For years. Till the war ended. And even when the war ended, I kept doing it, because nobody came to pick me up, say, you are my daughter or my cousin or my niece. There was-- no one survived, so nobody claimed me. And I had to-- my father was in America, but he could have been on the moon as far as I'm concerned. Because I didn't even know where America-- I knew America was there, and New York was there, in Polish it called Nev York. But I didn't know where there was. I didn't have any introduction to geography. I never learned geography. I had no idea how big, how far the ocean and how many oceans were between Poland and America or England or wherever. I didn't-- never saw a map before. And I knew he was far away in America, but how big the distance is. And I-- I had to go on doing the same thing what I was doing till the war-- after the war ended, because nobody came to pick me up, and nobody--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that the war had ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I knew. You knew, because you know what? The bombs flew the whole night on me that night. I was-- by the way, I was, that time-- oh, I have to continue. When I left that night, when I had to leave Grodzisko, when those Jewish people were being caught, the last Jewish people, wherever they were hiding, they kept finding out. And every time they caught a Jewish person, I felt it was getting closer to me. I-- I felt I was going to be next.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I realized, where am I to go? No-- I had no permanent place. So I decided I'll take a train to Przemysl. Why was I going to Przemysl? My heart was pulling me there, because that's where I left my-- my mother, my-- my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there for a year before-- in the war. Before the war I used to visit my uncle or whatever. I used to have happy memories there. I lived there for a year. I knew-- I had the address. I lived there before the war. And so that was the city I knew next best.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, where should I go? I went to the familiar places where I-- my chance of-- of-- of-- doing whatever I had to do, I knew there was better than other places where I didn't know anybody. Even though I didn't know anybody in Przemysl either, but I knew my surroundings a little better. So I take a train without money. I learned how to go without money and without being arrested. That was also a trick, without being arrested. I did it by avoiding conductor and getting to under-- instead of going where most people go in, in the main turnstile or whatever you go into, I went way under. I just pushed myself under, and I learned how to-- I hopped on the train, and there I go, and I was never arrested. I was lucky. That's why I said, I believe there was a Malakh next to me. How could you do this and never be arrested, doing this many times? So, I could hop the train, and I loved the train. I loved it. Till today, I loved that-- that whistle and the steam. And I loved the train going to Przemysl. That was exciting. What else did I have to do better? I mean, you know. And so, I went to Przemysl. Now, where do I go to Przemysl? I don't know anybody. So I went to the station, and I made myself comfortable. People were traveling back and forth sometimes during the night. You know, the train never closes because people are going during the night. And I sat myself in a corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's another thing. There was a conductor. He never arrested me. Never questioned me. And I was a poor little urchin. There were other urchins at that time because there was a war going on and other children lost their parents. They told me I should say I lost my parents in the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was a poor urchin, except deep inside I knew I wasn't just-- I was a Jewish child, and I had to save my life. And I had to do whatever it took to-- to-- whatever it took. You know? And so I faked my-- What did I do? Other people were sitting, sometimes picked up a newspaper. I didn't know how to read, so I picked up an old paper, and I looked there too, even though maybe it was upside down. I didn't know how to read. I was pretending I'm also reading, you know? And I didn't know how to read. That's what the role I was-- I was trying to play some role that I didn't know what I-- in other words, I was trying to survive. in the way I was faking some things. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was trying to be like, whatever I saw around me, I did the same thing. If I saw some-- of course I looked in the paper. I don't know how to read. And I was sitting, sometimes I fell asleep. It was warm. And that's how I--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I don't know how long I slept in that station. And then, I don't know if it was a night or, but how long can you sleep in a station? And-- and one day, as I'm going out of the station, I was looking around me where to go. I didn't know where I was going to go. So the conductor, who was standing in front, like a porter or whatever, he was standing in front of the station. He says, you know, I saw you too many times here. If I ever see you again, I'm going to arrest you.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he said that, I got very frightened, because I knew if I am arrested, this is the end of me. Because I knew I would never be able to even lie or cheat, because I don't know how. I didn't know how. I didn't know how to say a lie. I mean, a real lie, you know, like how to make up about myself something that wasn't. I didn't know how. I didn't have a second name, so how-- so anyway, I knew I would be found out, and I would be killed like a dog at the end of the war. It was almost towards the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know what? So that conductor did me a favor. Everything that happened, I think angels guarded me, because on the instant, I said, but where should I go? I put myself, so to speak, under his protection. I said, where should I go? I am-- I am an orphan. And tell me, where should I go? And he said, go down the street [SPEAKING POLISH]. I remember the street. The station was on [SPEAKING POLISH] in Przemysl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked down. He pointed this way, straight down. There are nuns there. They'll take you in. And I saw the end of my rope. I said, there was nothing else I could do. Because there-- you-- in-- you see in a city, it's not like you're up in a barn somewhere, where I knew. The-- the houses are closed at night. If you are seen, the police, they could arrest you. Police or whatever. So I figured this is the best thing under the circumstances. I showed him, I asked him, I said, how far, where, then I pointed. You go straight down there, and there you'll find the nuns. They'll take you in. And that's what I did. I walked straight there, and I walked-- came to the nuns, and I told them that I don't have where to go. And I don't remember everything clear, but I remember-- I don't remember whether I told them right away that I'm Jewish or later, but they knew I was Jewish. And they said they will take me in. And they will-- and so, they had to protect me. In other words, if I'm Jewish, I'll be in disguise. I'll-- I'll be a Polish child. And there were many other children there temporarily. Some of them had parents, and the parents came, one or two came to see them, but they were poor, probably, and they put them into the nuns. And I was there with the other. I slept. I had a bed for the few. It was maybe a few months. I don't believe it was longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to get back into a bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was feel great, but it-- let me tell you what the danger was. I was in great danger. I-- The danger was what I was always afraid. I had nightmares, and I would scream at night, and the other children heard the screams. They told me, you were screaming. You were saying something, and they didn't understand. Probably in Jewish. And they didn't understand, and that was the danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the nuns protected me. They told, maybe, the other children they shouldn't say anything to anybody. And then I found after I was a while, there were a few other Jewish children hidden there. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was one of a few. I don't remember their names, but I remember that I found out there were other few. Among the children, it-- you couldn't hide it completely. There is no way. Because the-- I had nightmares. I don't know if they had it, but I had nightmares.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I was afraid, but I had no place to go. And then, the nuns were as good as they could be. They didn't have much food, but the very, very, very, very minimum that you could survive on, they gave us. They gave, because there was very little food. There was a war going on, and the less they had, the less they could give the children, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But meanwhile, I think, you see, they were trying-- of course, as nuns, they had a duty to teach the children to be-- to show their religion to whoever was there, the Catholic religion. And after a while-- I-- see, I knew that I was Jewish, and I was brought up Orthodox and I knew a little bit of the shamar. So in the beginning, I used to say shamar, but after a year or two, I start forgetting it. And they were teaching me the Polish prayers, and I had to learn it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, first of all, because I was there, and I should look like other Polish children. And they didn't force us to become-- but they had to teach us that we should integrate. We should be like one of the other children. You know, they didn't force us to become Catholic or anything like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very kind nuns, and they were very good, and they were gentle and kind. And I went to look for them when I went back to Poland. I went to find them, but I couldn't find them. I wanted to give my thanks and know how they are. And what happened was, that it was-- since it was the end of the-- towards the end of the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the end of '44. That's when the Russians marched through that part of Poland. They liberated the end of '44. The closer you were to Russia, the earlier you were liberated. So not all Poland was liberated like one day or something. It took months, sometime, between one part. Sometimes they would fight a war. So for a long time. And they didn't get there till maybe 3/4 of a year or whatever. I don't know. I'll leave-- leave that to the historians that know dates.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I know is that my function was to survive. I remember winter and summer, when I was cold or warm or this. But I know now it was the end of '44. And how do I know it? Because I know, now, that it was '44. At that time, I didn't even know that it was '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so one day, since it was the end of the war, we went to a bunker with the nuns. We had to go under into a bunker that night, and the bombs kept falling and falling. And every bomb, I can still remember in my head today. I can still remember how it sounded, even though it was 50 years ago. The whole night, the bombs were so close that I thought they were on my head. Because every bomb that came, I can still hear the sound and the noise and I thought that was the last one that was going to hit us, and we will never make it. We were sitting crouched, the nuns with the children underneath. I don't know if it was underneath the church, or I don't remember exactly. But it was in a bunker. Now the bunker-- in front of the bunker, in the-- in the garden, so to speak. In the garden part. There were-- the Germans had-- had-- not just machine guns, but they had like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had-- they were shooting back against the Russians, because the Russians were-- were throwing down the bomb to win Poland back. But the Germans were not giving up, so they had these big cannon. Cannon. A small cannon. There were cannons standing right in the garden, and the cannon kept shooting up, and that's why there were-- because the cannons kept going as the planes were going through. They were trying to win the-- the war. The cannons were shooting up. And so that's why they knew how to find this-- and the bombs were so-- that every bomb that fell the whole night, I thought, that was the last time I'm going to be alive. It was a terrible, scary night. And when the morning came, suddenly there was quiet. And that's when we knew. I don't know. The nuns maybe walked out. And everybody knew. Whoever was in that, everybody knew, because you could-- just by the quietness, you knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- after a storm. Did you ever see a storm, and you walk out, and it's peace. After a storm? That's how it felt. It was quiet, and the Germans were gone. And everybody came out of there. Everybody, whatever was hiding, whether it was Jew or non-Jew. Everybody was out. They-- you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was walking in the street, I heard Jewish voices. There was the first morning, and I heard the first time, Jewish voices in front. I didn't go over to them, but I heard Jewish voices. Even though I didn't speak Yiddish well, but I still remembered it, and I could understand. And I heard Jewish voices, the first day. And I knew my life, I've won my freedom. My life is my own. That I finally made it to the day. You cannot imagine what that day felt like. [CRIES]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like [YIDDISH] came, you know? I was waiting for that day so long, so many bitter cold and frightening days. Frightening. And I finally reached that day that I was liberated. I think every person that was liberated felt like it was [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And even the goyim, they realized what a terrible thing. They thought the Germans are going to be good to them, but they-- they suffered too, because they had to give up food and the rations and everything. And they also had to-- you know, not like the Jews, but-- but-- but they also were glad that the Germans finally were gone and the Russians liberated. It was like a free. It was like everybody felt free. It was like-- I can't describe it. Even the trains, even the conductors were more lenient. Everybody went this way and that way, and people suddenly went traveling all over the place. It was unbelievable. It was-- I remember the day that they made the parade. The Russians marched, and there was a flag out of every house, in the front. There was flags flying. It felt like really liberation day. I remember that day. It was something-- I can't even describe to you how-- what happiness people felt, how finally this terrible war was over. And I was only a child, even then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when this-- on this day?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that day, I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 12. It was in '44, so I was 12. I was 12 years old. 12 years old after surviving the war. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I had no plan, and there was no plan for me. I still had to continue doing the same thing I did. I remember-- I don't remember clearly what I did day by day, but I remember an incident that like-- big incident that happened, because I had to still-- I don't remember where I went the first day, but I remember that I could survive. I was street smart by then. I was so street smart that I knew how to survive. It was unbelievable what I learned, you know, in those years. How to survive without-- without being taken care of. And I learned. And-- OK. And so I went one direction by train, and I went back to the village where I was born, right after. The first thing I did, go back. You know, the first-- I think I did go back. The first thing, I took a train, again without money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got to Grodzisko and to my familiar places, and I had to-- didn't have to be afraid of the Germans. But what I didn't know, that they made-- they made pogroms right after the war. And I found myself in Grodzisko. So what happened? The pogroms started in Kielce, and they were reverberated in every village there were Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Jewish people killed at this period after the war. If they came back to their own, I have several-- I have a story to tell. There was a family, a mother and a father that survived, that managed to be hidden by-- by the way, I have to mention, they were hidden by the [POLISH], which is in Polish, but in English, it's like the mayor, equivalent to a mayor. And he-- I thought he was anti-Semitic. He had such a name, and he happened to be the brother who was the barber to me, but the brother was a barber, and the other brother was a-- the [POLISH], which is the mayor. And they happened to live near the-- next to the main road. And it so happened that they hid a family. And they were one of the few people that hid the entire family of three, even though he saw the Nazi every day. He had to be in contact with it, because he represented the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So nobody suspected him. And yet, one day, I remember, I have to go back. When during the war, I was wandering, one day I happened to walk into the village, close. It was very dangerous. And I wasn't afraid by then. I was so sure that I'll survive. But I didn't go into the center, but I went very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I happened to go into his house. I don't remember how I got. And the wife, she did the kindest-- I didn't even need it, but she let me sleep that night in-- I don't know, her bed. I wasn't even clean. She gave me some clothing, and she gave me some food, and the next day I left. But I didn't know that they were hiding a whole family. The only whole family that survived in the village. They were hiding this family. The [POLISH]. And I think I even remember somewhere in my brain, they had-- but I can't tell you right this second. Baye. Now I remember. The name was Baye. And that was the name. And he-- they hid the Jewish family. The name was Fingerhut. And they hid this family, and they were quite well-off before the war. And the child was younger than me. I used to play with her sometimes before the war, because she was an only child, and she had dolls and beautiful things. You know, like I didn't have that kind of a doll, and I was envious of her because she had that doll. And I used to know this child, and she was younger-- young. Quite a few younger than me. And she was never left alone like I did. She-- her parents, you know, through the war, they were hiding. But they survived. And when the war ended, I went back to the Jewish family. They went back to their house, and I-- the last time I visited that village before I permanently left there. So I showed my face there. And when I came to their house, she-- she saw me. She talked to me. But she didn't say, you can sleep in my house or whatever. But the father must have gone. The husband must have-- he must have wrote-- he must have wrote a letter to his sister in America, and the letter appeared in 1945. The early part of '45. I have the-- the article. The whole page that it appeared. The paragraph appeared about me in the paper when the war was still going on in Poland. And he wrote to his sister. In those days, people wrote already when the war was-- as soon as the war was ended in a part of Poland, somehow they managed to get it, either through the [NON-ENGLISH] or another way, and they sent it to America, and it appeared in the paper, in the forewords in the Jewish paper. They were searching people from their own relatives, if they had relatives in New York or any place in America. And that article about me, the paragraph, appeared in the page where people were searching for their relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
